author: @brotolero
text: something were wrong with the measurement here. I downloaded the design  fix a bit the aspect ratio ( more taller, and less diameter, ( more like the optional design in the thinkercad  addrees) and i got less than 600 rpm for a wind of 3.7m/s. More according to clasical savonius. The best design that i tested until the moment is the clasical schultz rotor of 3 blades at 120º.  The ugrinsky design seek the same idea shorten the distance betwen blades to down the drag of the returning blade. I printed also the ugrinsky and dint beat the schultz rotor, and if you use only one section of ugrinsky (not the whole screw) can found exist a position of the blades where the drag forces are equilibrated and the turbine dint begin to spin. And that not is eficience for me. Should use other tachometer or find what were wromg with the measurement. Because in the video dont seem the turbine would be spining at 2600 rpm neither. Being said that, i saw a lot of your videos and your way to produce theys is so good  and most technical compared to others. Continue bringing enjoy to the planet with your enthusiasm.

author: @matthewbecker-mdcclxxvi
text:
At 2:40, you say that you are getting 1.1 or 1.2 volts. One thing that confuses me in your recent turbine series... You always talk about how many volts are produced. But what good is a volt without amps? I always have thought that it was watts that we wanted as the ultimate outcome, not mere volts. While you do talk about "milliwatts" later on (lolz, but seriously, good job!), it is way too common that I hear discussion in the context of turbines about volts without any mention of amps and/or watts.

Could someone please explain to me what I am missing here? Is turbine based power entirely volt-fluctuations with otherwise constant amps? Or do amps change also depending on certain circumstances?

author: @kadmow
text: So with the high tip speed ratio this is acting as a self starting aerodynamic device - not an impulse or drag generator.  The drag component only comes into effect once the TSR is about or below 1 (ie, slower than the wind - when loaded up the device may convert to a higher torque drag (not truly impulse) device, while at high TSR it  appears to be a velocity - aerodynamic - machine (lower torque).)

author: @unicornadrian1358
text: Pelton wheels are used in conjunction with a nozzle to redirect all the water onto the cups. Having a funnel to direct the wind onto the correct part of the turbine might improve performance, especially in low wind. Maybe a profile similar to a Tesla turbine intake, box tapering to a slot?

replies.author: @chriskeeble
replies.text: I think part of the idea behind this type of design is that it can capture the wind as it changes direction, even rapidly, without gyroscopic effect stresses, etc. seen in other types of turbine that have to turn into the wind to capture it.

replies.author: @unicornadrian1358
replies.text: @chriskeeble that wouldn’t be a problem with a wind concentrator to accelerate the wind. Four of them around the turbine would account for wind from any direction

replies.author: @chriskeeble
replies.text: @unicornadrian1358 it would be interesting to see what effect this would have, for sure. My own (relatively ignorant) guess would be very little, given the fins in the pelton design Rob has produced. But a proper test would be interesting if anyone has the time, equipment and inclination to give it a go and share.

author: @danedmiston9673
text:
The RPM meter is off by about a factor of 10.  A meter that "sees" only retro-reflective tape will give much more accurate readings.
Assuming your video is shot at 30 frames per second I count 4.3 revolutions per second or 258 RPM.
With a rotor diameter of .14 meters  at 258 RPM in 4 MPS wind gives a TSR of about 0.46.  Which is about right for drag turbine.
The question came up, does one wobble equal one revolution?  If a shaft is bent; yes. If the bearing or shaft mounts are sloppy; no.
I looked at the video again and noticed a light reflecting off of the reflective tape about once every four frames.  That would indicate an RPM of about 450 and a 0.8 TSR.  (I think the bearing or shaft mounts are sloppy.)

Guess it’s possible he’s seeing aliasing - maybe original author can confirm his method?

author: @onbedoeldekut1515
text:
Where you're losing massive massive potential is by just having air scoops.

If each scoop was instead a stationary funnel (like a vintage hearing trumpet)  pointing toward a central rotating scoop array, you would effectively be increasing the airspeed through the funnels until it hits the scoops.

You could even maximise the potential by having the entire exterior comprised of the funnels, so that any air is channelled toward the scoops, not losing a single mote of energy, unlike your horrendously wasteful example!
(I'm joking)
